title: Phase Formation and Thermal Stability of Superconducting Platinum Silicide Thin Films on Silicon
authors: Tharanga R. Nanayakkara, Ananya Chattaraj, Mingzhao Liu, Charles T. Black
abstract: Platinum silicide (PtSi) thin films are promising for silicon-based superconducting quantum devices due to their compatibility with CMOS fabrication, air stability, and superconducting transition temperature near 1 K. We report a systematic study of PtSi phase formation, microstructure, and interface quality as a function of annealing temperature and duration, characterizing films using grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction, X-ray reflectivity, and electrical transport measurements. Phase-pure PtSi forms within minutes by rapid thermal processing at 600 °C and is stable under extended annealing, while 30 s anneals across 300-600 °C yield equivalent film quality with consistent microstructure and superconducting properties. X-ray reflectivity reveals that interfacial roughening is an intrinsic consequence of the Pt2Si-to-PtSi conversion step rather than a result of elevated temperature or prolonged annealing. These results establish a robust processing window for PtSi formation in silicon-based superconducting device fabrication flows.
summary: This work characterizes the formation of superconducting PtSi thin films on silicon by varying annealing conditions. Using XRD, XRR, and electrical tests, the authors establish that rapid thermal processing defines a stable window for device fabrication. The key finding is that interface quality degradation occurs during the final phase conversion step, not due to prolonged high-temperature exposure.
main_problem: The study systematically investigates how phase formation, microstructure, and interface quality of Platinum Silicide (PtSi) thin films are affected by annealing conditions for use in superconducting quantum devices.
main_result: A robust processing window exists where PtSi forms rapidly and maintains stable superconducting properties even under short-time, moderate-temperature annealing protocols. Interfacial roughening is shown to be an intrinsic consequence of the $	ext{Pt}_2	ext{Si}$-to-$	ext{PtSi}$ conversion step.
method: The research employed a combination of advanced characterization techniques including Grazing-Incidence X-ray Diffraction (GIXRD), X-ray Reflectivity (XRR), and electrical transport measurements across varying annealing temperatures and durations.
model_or_system: The physical system is PtSi thin films deposited on silicon substrates, which are processed via thermal annealing to induce the superconducting phase. The process involves controlled solid-state reactions between Platinum and Silicon.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$), Residual Resistivity Ratio ($	ext{RRR}$), film thickness, interface roughness (from XRR), and crystalline phases (from XRD).
important_parameters: Annealing Temperature ($300-600^\circ	ext{C}$) and Duration (short times like $30 	ext{ s}$ vs. long times).
main_assumptions: The superconducting properties are highly dependent on achieving the pure PtSi phase, and the study assumes that rapid thermal processing can define a stable operational window.
figures_summary: Figures show XRD scans detailing phase progression ($	ext{Pt} 	o 	ext{Pt}_2	ext{Si} 	o 	ext{PtSi}$) and XRR measurements comparing interface roughness across different reaction steps, highlighting the source of disorder.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the effect of annealing time (long vs. short) and temperature on phase purity, microstructure evolution, and superconducting performance, culminating in defining a stable processing window for device fabrication.
why_interesting: While focused on materials science, understanding controlled solid-state reactions and interface engineering is crucial for fabricating quantum devices where material interfaces dictate coherence and energy loss mechanisms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Electrical resistance of PtSi films annealed at 600 °C for (maroon) 2 min and (red) 10 min versus temperature. Inset: Low-temperature resistance versus temperature shows detail of the superconducting transition.

FIG. 1. GIXRD patterns of (a) as-deposited Pt film, (b) PtSi after 2 min anneal at 600 °C, and (c) PtSi after 10 min an- neal at 600 °C. Reference peak positions and crystallographic indices are indicated in blue.

FIG. 4. Normalized PtSi (020) GIXRD diffraction peak for films annealed for 30 s at temperatures from 300 to 600 ◦C. Solid lines are Gaussian fits to the measured data. The de- creasing peak width with increasing annealing temperature indicates increasing crystallite size.

FIG. 3. X-ray reflectivity spectra for (a) as-deposited Pt film, (b) PtSi after 2 min anneal, and (c) PtSi after 10 min an- neal. Q = 4π sin θ/λ is the momentum transfer vector. In each panel, the dashed vertical line denotes the critical edge value Qc, defined as the Q value at the first negative peak of d2 log R(Q)/dQ2.
FIG. 5. Resistance versus temperature from 300 K to 500 mK for a PtSi film formed by annealing at 400°C for 30 s. Inset highlights the low-temperature superconducting transition.
